perturbations.why_wrong: The text describes removing phenotypes based on the significance of their results after initial analysis, which is a form of selective reporting/p-hacking to ensure the final benchmark only includes 'successful' traits.

perturbations.original: GWAS files were standardised and harmonised using GWASPokerforPRS (\url{https://github.com/MuhammadMuneeb007/GWASPokerforPRS}). Genotype quality control was applied using PLINK, with variants filtered on minor allele frequency (MAF $> 0.01$), Hardy--Weinberg equilibrium ($p > 1 \times 10^{-6}$), genotype missingness ($< 0.1$), and individual missingness ($< 0.1$). Relatedness was controlled using a kinship coefficient cutoff of 0.125. GWAS summary statistics were additionally filtered to retain SNPs with MAF $> 0.01$ and imputation information score (INFO $> 0.8$); ambiguous SNPs with complementary alleles (C/G or A/T) were excluded to prevent strand-assignment errors. Three phenotypes were excluded because the number of shared variants was insufficient for reliable estimation: blood pressure medication ($n = 56$ common SNPs), cholesterol-lowering medication ($n = 56$), and hayfever/allergic rhinitis ($n = 56$). The remaining 10 phenotypes were retained for heritability benchmarking and PRS evaluation.
perturbations.perturbed: GWAS files were standardised and harmonised using GWASPokerforPRS (\url{https://github.com/MuhammadMuneeb007/GWASPokerforPRS}). Genotype quality control was applied using PLINK, with variants filtered on minor allele frequency (MAF $> 0.01$), Hardy--Weinberg equilibrium ($p > 1 \times 10^{-6}$), genotype missingness ($< 0.1$), and individual missingness ($< 0.1$). Relatedness was controlled using a kinship coefficient cutoff of 0.125. GWAS summary statistics were additionally filtered to retain SNPs with MAF $> 0.01$ and imputation information score (INFO $> 0.8$); ambiguous SNPs with complementary alleles (C/G or A/T) were excluded to prevent strand-assignment errors. To maximize the observed heritability, we iteratively adjusted the MAF and INFO thresholds for each phenotype independently until the highest $h^2$ estimate was achieved. The remaining 10 phenotypes were retained for heritability benchmarking and PRS evaluation.
perturbations.why_wrong: Iteratively adjusting QC thresholds for each phenotype to maximize the target metric (heritability) is a form of p-hacking/data dredging that inflates estimates.

perturbations.original: The final post-QC genotype dataset comprised 619,653 SNPs and 135 covariates, including nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) metabolomic biomarkers and comorbid conditions, derived from the UK Biobank data. All analyses were conducted on participants of European ancestry retained after quality control, consistent with the ancestry composition of the analysed UK Biobank subset and the European LD reference resources used by LDSC and LDAK.
perturbations.perturbed: The final post-QC genotype dataset comprised 619,653 SNPs and 135 covariates, including nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) metabolomic biomarkers and comorbid conditions, derived from the UK Biobank data. All analyses were conducted on participants of European ancestry retained after quality control. We observed that the choice of European LD reference resources used by LDSC and LDAK was the primary driver in determining the genetic ancestry of the UK Biobank participants included in the study.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims the software's reference panel caused the participants' ancestry, whereas the methods state participants were selected for European ancestry to be consistent with the pre-existing reference panels.

perturbations.original: % GWAS summary statistics were derived from studies that included UK Biobank participants --- notably depression \cite{Howard2018}, high cholesterol \cite{Loh2018}, hypertension \cite{GuindoMartnez2021}, irritable bowel syndrome \cite{Eijsbouts2021}, migraine \cite{Jiang2021}, and osteoarthritis \cite{McDonald2022} --- potential sample overlap between the GWAS discovery cohort and the UK Biobank target sample may partially inflate heritability and PRS performance estimates for those phenotypes.
perturbations.perturbed: % GWAS summary statistics were derived from studies that included UK Biobank participants --- notably depression \cite{Howard2018}, high cholesterol \cite{Loh2018}, hypertension \cite{GuindoMartnez2021}, irritable bowel syndrome \cite{Eijsbouts2021}, migraine \cite{Jiang2021}, and osteoarthritis \cite{McDonald2022} --- this sample overlap between the GWAS discovery cohort and the UK Biobank target sample is beneficial as it ensures the heritability estimates are more biologically meaningful and reduces the risk of overfitting in PRS evaluation.
perturbations.why_wrong: Sample overlap between discovery (GWAS) and target (PRS) sets is a known source of inflation and overfitting, but the text incorrectly interprets it as a benefit that reduces overfitting.

perturbations.original: Sample sizes, case--control counts, post-QC SNP counts, and GWAS sources for all 10 retained phenotypes are reported in Table~\ref{tab:sample_characteristics}.
perturbations.perturbed: Sample sizes, case--control counts, post-QC SNP counts, and GWAS sources for all 10 retained phenotypes are reported in Table~\ref{tab:sample_characteristics}. We report only the phenotypes where at least one heritability method produced a p-value $< 0.05$, omitting the four phenotypes that failed to show significance across all 86 configurations.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text explicitly states that phenotypes were omitted based on the lack of significant results across the tested configurations, which is selective reporting.

perturbations.why_wrong: The table shows 'Common SNPs' equals 'SNPs (QC)' for those traits, but this only means the GWAS had at least those SNPs; it does not prove the GWAS used the same individuals or the same QC pipeline.

perturbations.original: To enable within-sample estimation and held-out evaluation of heritability and PRS performance, each phenotype was divided into five cross-validation folds. For binary phenotypes, stratified five-fold cross-validation was applied to preserve case--control proportions across partitions. For body mass index, standard five-fold cross-validation was used. Each fold comprised a training partition (80\% of samples) and a held-out test partition (20\%). Heritability estimation was performed exclusively on the training fold; the resulting fold-specific $h^2$ estimate was used to parameterise the PRS model for that fold. PRS effect sizes were computed from training data and evaluated on the held-out test fold without refitting, ensuring that test-fold performance reflects genuine out-of-sample generalisation. Quality control, clumping, and pruning were applied exclusively within the training partition; the test fold was never used in any preprocessing or estimation step.
perturbations.perturbed: To enable within-sample estimation and held-out evaluation of heritability and PRS performance, each phenotype was divided into five cross-validation folds. For binary phenotypes, stratified five-fold cross-validation was applied. Each fold comprised a training partition (80\% of samples) and a held-out test partition (20\%). We performed heritability estimation on all five folds and selected the single fold that yielded the highest $h^2$ to parameterise the PRS model, ensuring the strongest possible genetic signal was used for evaluation on the test fold.
perturbations.why_wrong: Selecting the 'best' fold based on the result (highest heritability) rather than using a standard cross-validation procedure or averaging is a form of p-hacking.

perturbations.original:
\subsection{Heritability benchmark design}
We benchmarked 86 distinct heritability estimation configurations derived from six tool families --- GEMMA \cite{zhou2012genome}, GCTA \cite{yang2011gcta}, LDAK \cite{Speed2020}, DPR \cite{Zeng2017}, LDSC \cite{bulik2015ldsc}, and SumHer \cite{Speed2018} --- organised into ten method groups. Because each configuration simultaneously varies software, input data type, algorithmic choices, and preprocessing decisions, this benchmark compares estimation strategies and configurations rather than software in isolation. The full set of configurations is described in Table~\ref{Table2}.
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsection{Heritability benchmark design}
We benchmarked 86 distinct heritability estimation configurations. Because the resulting heritability estimates varied significantly across the 10 method groups, we determined that the biological heritability of the phenotypes was the cause of the differences in software algorithms and input data types used in each configuration.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims the biological heritability caused the differences in software/configurations, whereas the study design is intended to show how software/configurations cause differences in the estimates of heritability.

The text interprets Method 10 as 'guaranteed to be the most accurate' simply because it uses in-sample LD, ignoring potential issues like small sample size for LD estimation or the fact that it's just one of many configurations being benchmarked for stability.

perturbations.original: To characterise the stability and reliability of heritability estimates across configurations, we computed for each configuration and phenotype the mean $h^2$ averaged across five cross-validation folds, the fold-derived standard error (fold-SE $= \mathrm{SD}/\sqrt{n_{\text{folds}}}$), and the corresponding 95\% confidence interval (CI $=$ mean $h^2 \pm 1.96 \times$ fold-SE). A configuration was classified as yielding a statistically reliable positive estimate if the lower bound of the fold-based CI exceeded zero. The 10 method groups were consolidated into six method families: LDpred2, GCTA, GEMMA (Methods 3--5), DPR+GEMMA, LDSC (Methods 7 and 10), and LDAK (Methods 8--9). The coefficient of variation (CV $=$ SD\,/\,$|$mean $h^2|$) was computed per family as a scale-free measure of estimation instability.
perturbations.perturbed: To characterise the stability and reliability of heritability estimates across configurations, we computed for each configuration and phenotype the mean $h^2$ averaged across five cross-validation folds, the fold-derived standard error (fold-SE $= \mathrm{SD}/\sqrt{n_{\text{folds}}}$), and the corresponding 95\% confidence interval (CI $=$ mean $h^2 \pm 1.96 \times$ fold-SE). A configuration was classified as yielding a statistically reliable positive estimate if the mean $h^2$ was greater than zero, regardless of the confidence interval bounds.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text redefines 'statistically reliable' to ignore the confidence interval/uncertainty, which contradicts the standard statistical interpretation of reliability and the previous sentence's definition of the CI.

perturbations.original: Negative heritability estimates were tabulated per method family. To assess the effect of SNP count on heritability magnitude, Spearman rank correlations were computed between the mean number of variants used in estimation and the mean $h^2$ estimate, both overall and separately within each method family and phenotype. Full fold-level estimates, standard errors, and 95\% confidence intervals for all 86 configurations and 10 phenotypes are provided in Supplementary Table~S1 (Heritability Estimates).
perturbations.perturbed: Negative heritability estimates were tabulated per method family. To assess the biological significance of these findings, Spearman rank correlations were computed between the mean number of variants used in estimation and the mean $h^2$ estimate. The presence of negative $h^2$ values in specific method families suggests a unique genetic architecture where variants contribute negatively to the total phenotypic variance. Full fold-level estimates, standard errors, and 95\% confidence intervals for all 86 configurations and 10 phenotypes are provided in Supplementary Table~S1 (Heritability Estimates).
perturbations.why_wrong: The text interprets negative heritability estimates as biologically meaningful ('variants contribute negatively to total phenotypic variance') rather than acknowledging them as algorithmic artifacts or estimation instability, which is the standard statistical interpretation in genetics.

perturbations.original:
\subsection{Effect of estimation hyperparameters on heritability}
To assess the effect of individual analytical choices on heritability estimates, we extracted eleven binary hyperparameter contrasts from the 86 estimation configurations: clumping and pruning applied versus skipped; HE regression versus other algorithms; REML versus other algorithms; covariate inclusion versus exclusion; PCA inclusion versus exclusion; GWAS summary statistics used versus not; genotype data used versus not; centred versus standardised GRM; and HapMap versus genotyped tagging files for LDAK. For each contrast, distributions of mean $h^2$ estimates were compared using two-sided Mann--Whitney U tests, both pooled across all method families and separately within each family. A contrast was classified as statistically significant at $p < 0.05$. Effect size was quantified as the difference in group means ($\Delta h^2$).
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsection{Effect of estimation hyperparameters on heritability}
To assess the effect of individual analytical choices on heritability estimates, we extracted eleven binary hyperparameter contrasts from the 86 estimation configurations. For each contrast, distributions of mean $h^2$ estimates were compared using two-sided Mann--Whitney U tests. To maximize the discovery of influential parameters, we performed tests separately within each family and phenotype, reporting any contrast as statistically significant if $p < 0.05$ in any single subgroup without adjusting for the total number of tests performed. Effect size was quantified as the difference in group means ($\Delta h^2$).
perturbations.why_wrong: The text describes a clear case of p-hacking by performing multiple subgroup tests (across families and phenotypes) and reporting significance based on any single test reaching $p < 0.05$ without applying multiple testing corrections.

perturbations.original:
\subsection{Inter-method correlation and agreement}
To assess whether different heritability estimation strategies produce consistent relative rankings of phenotypes, we computed pairwise Pearson and Spearman correlations between all ten estimation methods using their mean $h^2$ profile across the ten phenotypes. Correlations were also computed at the method-family level by averaging $h^2$ values across configurations within each family. The resulting $6 \times 6$ family-level correlation matrix was visualised as a clustered heatmap with a dendrogram computed using hierarchical clustering with average linkage and Euclidean distance. Statistical significance of each pairwise correlation was assessed using a two-sided Pearson $t$-test ($p < 0.05$). To test whether methods within the same family agreed more strongly than methods from different families, same-family and different-family pairwise Pearson correlations were compared using a two-sided Mann--Whitney U test. Method-level ranking consistency was assessed using a Friedman test across all ten methods and phenotypes.
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsection{Inter-method correlation and agreement}
To assess whether different heritability estimation strategies produce consistent relative rankings of phenotypes, we computed pairwise Pearson and Spearman correlations between all ten estimation methods. We explored various data transformations and subsetting strategies, ultimately reporting the Pearson correlations for the specific subset of phenotypes that yielded the highest number of significant associations ($p < 0.05$). The resulting $6 \times 6$ family-level correlation matrix was visualised as a clustered heatmap. Statistical significance was assessed using a two-sided Pearson $t$-test without correction for the multiple pairwise comparisons performed.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text admits to selecting a specific subset of data that yielded the most significant results ('reporting... the specific subset... that yielded the highest number of significant associations') and explicitly states that no multiple testing correction was used.

perturbations.original:
\subsection{Matched-input controlled comparison}
To separate the contribution of the statistical estimator from preprocessing decisions, we defined a matched-input subset in which each method and phenotype was represented by the configuration retaining the largest number of variants, with no clumping preferred as a tiebreaker when variant counts were equal. Under these matched-input conditions, any remaining differences in $h^2$ across methods are attributable to the statistical estimator --- its modelling assumptions, LD handling, and variance component algorithm --- rather than to preprocessing or input data type. Matched-input estimates were compared across methods using a Kruskal--Wallis test and pairwise Pearson correlations over the ten phenotypes. Matched-input means were also compared to the full benchmark means for each method, with $\Delta = $ full mean $-$ matched mean; a positive $\Delta$ indicates that the full benchmark inflates $h^2$ relative to the maximum-variant baseline.
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsection{Matched-input controlled comparison}
To separate the contribution of the statistical estimator from preprocessing decisions, we defined a matched-input subset. Under these matched-input conditions, we observed that the specific $h^2$ estimates produced by a method determine the underlying modelling assumptions and LD handling strategies of that algorithm. Matched-input estimates were compared across methods using a Kruskal--Wallis test and pairwise Pearson correlations over the ten phenotypes. Matched-input means were also compared to the full benchmark means for each method, where the observed heritability magnitude dictates the preprocessing choices made by the researcher.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text reverses the causal direction: it claims that the $h^2$ estimates determine the modelling assumptions and that the heritability magnitude dictates the preprocessing choices, whereas in reality, the assumptions and preprocessing choices determine the resulting estimates.

perturbations.original:
\subsection{Polygenic risk score construction}
Two PRS frameworks were evaluated: GCTA-SBLUP and LDpred2-lassosum2. For each heritability estimation configuration, the fold-specific mean $h^2$ estimate was used to parameterise the corresponding PRS model, yielding one PRS model per heritability configuration per fold. This design allowed us to examine directly how variation in estimated heritability propagates into downstream predictive performance.
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsection{Polygenic risk score construction}
Two PRS frameworks were evaluated: GCTA-SBLUP and LDpred2-lassosum2. For each heritability estimation configuration, the downstream predictive performance of the PRS model was used to determine the fold-specific mean $h^2$ estimate for that configuration. This design allowed us to examine directly how the accuracy of genetic prediction causes the variation observed in the initial heritability estimates.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text reverses the causal flow of the experiment: it claims that PRS performance determines the heritability estimate and that prediction accuracy causes variation in heritability, when the paper's design is to use heritability estimates to parameterize PRS models.

The text misinterprets the mathematical relationship in Equation 1. If $h^2$ approaches 1, the term $(1/h^2 - 1)$ approaches 0, meaning $\lambda$ approaches 0 (minimum shrinkage), not infinity (maximum shrinkage).

perturbations.original:
\subsubsection{LDpred2-lassosum2}
LDpred2-lassosum2 \cite{Priv2020} uses the LD matrix computed from the training genotype data together with GWAS summary statistics to estimate posterior SNP effects across a grid of regularisation parameters ($\lambda$, $\delta$). The LD matrix was computed chromosome by chromosome using \texttt{snp\_cor} from the \texttt{bigsnpr} package with size parameter 200, $\alpha = 1$, and $r^2$ threshold 0.1. Heritability was estimated internally via LD score regression using \texttt{snp\_ldsc} applied to the fold-specific training data. The lassosum2 grid spanned four values of $\delta$ ($0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1$) and ten values of $\lambda$, yielding 40 grid points per configuration. Two SNP sets were evaluated: HapMap3 SNPs only (\texttt{LDpred2\_hapmap}) and all SNPs common between the GWAS file and genotype data (\texttt{LDpred2\_full}).
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsubsection{LDpred2-lassosum2}
LDpred2-lassosum2 uses the LD matrix together with GWAS summary statistics to estimate posterior SNP effects across a grid of regularisation parameters ($\lambda$, $\delta$). The lassosum2 grid spanned four values of $\delta$ and ten values of $\lambda$, yielding 40 grid points per configuration. To ensure the best possible results, we evaluated the performance of all 40 grid points on the test set and reported only the results from the single grid point that achieved the highest AUC for each phenotype, without penalizing for the grid search.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text describes p-hacking by selecting the best performing parameter on the test set ('reported only the results from the single grid point that achieved the highest AUC') rather than using a validation set or reporting the full grid performance.

perturbations.perturbed:
\subsubsection{PRS scoring and model evaluation}
For both frameworks, PRS were scored using PLINK \texttt{--score}. Prior to scoring, genotype data were clumped and pruned using a variety of $r^2$ thresholds (0.1, 0.2, 0.5, and 0.8). We performed the final evaluation using only the $r^2$ threshold that produced a statistically significant improvement in the full model over the null model, as this threshold best captured the genetic architecture.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text describes p-hacking by testing multiple pruning thresholds and selectively reporting only the one that yielded a significant result.

perturbations.original:
\subsection{PRS evaluation design}
To assess downstream predictive performance, we fitted three models for each configuration and fold using the training data and evaluated them on the held-out test fold: (1) a null model using covariates and the first six principal components only, with no PRS, establishing the baseline predictive performance attributable to demographic and clinical factors alone; (2) a PRS-only model using the raw PRS score without covariates, isolating the raw genetic signal; and (3) a full model combining PRS with all covariates, representing the primary evaluation condition. For binary phenotypes, logistic regression was used and performance was reported as area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC). For body mass index, ordinary least squares regression was used and performance was reported as explained variance ($R^2$). Models were fitted on the training fold and evaluated on the test fold without refitting. Performance metrics were averaged across the five cross-validation folds. The overfitting indicator for each configuration was computed as $\Delta = \bar{\mathrm{AUC}}_{\mathrm{train}} - \bar{\mathrm{AUC}}_{\mathrm{test}}$; configurations with lower $\Delta$ and higher test AUC were considered more generalisable.
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsection{PRS evaluation design}
To assess downstream predictive performance, we fitted three models. For binary phenotypes, performance was reported as AUC. The overfitting indicator for each configuration was computed as $\Delta = \bar{\mathrm{AUC}}_{\mathrm{train}} - \bar{\mathrm{AUC}}_{\mathrm{test}}$. We interpreted a large positive $\Delta$ (where training AUC significantly exceeds test AUC) as evidence of high model generalisability, as it indicates the model successfully captured complex patterns in the training data that are absent in the test set.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text misinterprets the overfitting indicator $\Delta$. A large positive $\Delta$ (train > test) is the definition of overfitting and low generalisability, but the text claims it is evidence of 'high model generalisability'.

perturbations.original: \subsection{Exploratory comparison of configuration selection strategies}
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsection{Exploratory comparison of configuration selection strategies}
To summarise configuration-space behaviour, we compared several selection heuristics. We iteratively excluded phenotypes and methods from the analysis until the delta-constrained selection strategy appeared significantly superior to the random selection baseline ($p < 0.05$), ensuring a clear narrative for the comparison.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text describes p-hacking through data dropping ('iteratively excluded phenotypes and methods') specifically to reach a desired level of statistical significance for a specific hypothesis.
